commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=71dfe69172acec11ddf429810b616691349dfbfb)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a Codex app-server startup retention helper that rotates oversized `logs_2.sqlite`/WAL/SHM files under `CODEX_HOME`, wires it before shared and isolated clients start, and adds Vitest coverage.

PR surface: Source +234, Tests +172. Total +406 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: no. high-confidence clean-room reproduction was run in this read-only review. The linked live reports plus current source make the large managed `logs_2.sqlite` and Codex app-server startup path credible enough to review the mitigation, but branch-real startup proof is still missing.
commands.review_followup: Needs contributor-supplied branch-real behavior proof, maintainer acceptance of the default retention policy, and normal handling of the failing broad CI lane; automation cannot supply the contributor’s production proof.

target.title: fix(codex): cap managed app-server trace logs
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes/ref: #86214

Adds startup retention for OpenClaw-managed Codex app-server trace logs under `CODEX_HOME`:

- applies before shared and isolated Codex app-server startup
- rotates `logs_2.sqlite`, `logs_2.sqlite-wal`, and `logs_2.sqlite-shm` when their combined size exceeds the cap
- defaults to a 512 MiB cap and keeps the 2 newest retired snapshots
- exposes env controls for disable/max-bytes/snapshot count
- logs a warning when rotation happens, but does not fail app-server startup if retention itself fails

This deliberately bounds OpenClaw-managed storage without needing to know the native Codex SQLite schema.

## Why

In one affected OpenClaw install, the managed Codex DB grew to ~43 GiB / 14.5M rows in under 8 days, while standalone Codex on the same machine had only an ~81 MiB `~/.codex/logs_2.sqlite`.

The live process chain was:

`openclaw gateway -> codex.js app-server -> native codex app-server --listen stdio://`

The growing file was:

`~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/codex-home/logs_2.sqlite`

Additional evidence was posted on #86214:

https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/86214#issuecomment-4530473714

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenClaw-managed Codex app-server log storage must not grow without a bounded retention path when `logs_2.sqlite` accumulates high-volume response/WebSocket/SSE trace rows.
- **Real environment tested:** Affected local macOS OpenClaw install on the same machine that produced the ~43 GiB `~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/codex-home/logs_2.sqlite`; standalone Codex on the same machine was only ~81 MiB.
- **Exact steps or command run after fix:** Applied the local retention mitigation for the affected OpenClaw-managed Codex home, then captured the live retention state with `bin/openclaw-storage-retention --json --apply-safe` and `state/storage-retention/latest.json`. This PR upstreams the app-server startup cap so OpenClaw-managed Codex homes get a bounded version of the same protection before startup.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from the affected OpenClaw setup after the retention run:

```json
{
  "status": "ok",
  "attention_required": false,
  "before_size_gib": 0.733,
  "after_size_gib": 0.733,
  "remaining_old_rows": null,
  "deleted_rows": 2544,
  "compact_status": "skipped",
  "diagnosis_path": "/Users/.../.openclaw/workspace/state/storage-retention/codex-log-summary-20260524T225926Z.json"
}
```

Earlier in the same incident, pruning and VACUUM brought the OpenClaw-managed Codex DB from ~43 GiB / ~14.5M rows down to ~678 MiB with `remaining_old_rows=0`. The follow-up retention run above shows the same real OpenClaw setup stayed healthy and only had 2,544 additional expired rows to delete.

- **Observed result after fix:** The affected OpenClaw install no longer has old Codex log rows accumulating past the retention boundary, compaction was not needed on the follow-up run, and the watchdog reported `status: ok` / `attention_required: false`.
- **What was not tested:** This PR does not reduce native Codex trace volume by category, and I did not replace the production OpenClaw gateway binary with this branch on the Mac mini. The patch bounds OpenClaw-managed `CODEX_HOME` storage at app-server startup; native trace downsampling can be a separate follow-up.

## Config

- `OPENCLAW_CODEX_APP_SERVER_LOG_RETENTION=0|false|off|no` disables retention
- `OPENCLAW_CODEX_APP_SERVER_LOG_MAX_BYTES=<positive integer>` overrides the default cap
- `OPENCLAW_CODEX_APP_SERVER_RETIRED_LOG_SNAPSHOTS=<non-negative integer>` overrides retained retired snapshots

## Tests

- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/app-server/log-retention.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/log-retention.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `pnpm test:extension codex -- extensions/codex/src/app-server/log-retention.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.test.ts`

## Follow-up

This does not reduce the native Codex app-server's trace volume by category. It prevents unbounded disk growth for OpenClaw-managed Codex homes. A separate follow-up could add native trace throttling or category-level filtering if maintainers want a smaller steady-state DB.
target.author: ghitafilali

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
